what_it_does: This file is a research-and-decision report that surveys OpenWebUI customization options and code-level extension mechanisms, then evaluates each against three fabric-integration requirements: outbound calls from OpenWebUI to an external multi-agent fabric, inbound push from the fabric into the UI, and a members/channels panel. It recommends a no-fork architecture built on OpenWebUI's native Channels substrate, a Pipe Function, and the undocumented channel inbound webhook.
observations: Evidence is grounded in a local pip install of open-webui v0.8.12 source, with file and line references.
The likely deploy target uses an unpinned `ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui:main` image, creating a version-drift risk against the verified v0.8.12 source.
Live docs track a newer release (~v0.9.x), so some documented features may differ from v0.8.12.
A TL;DR decision table maps the three requirements to best-fit mechanisms and whether a fork is needed.
OpenWebUI's native Channels feature already provides real-time messaging, a members list, threads, reactions, pinned messages, and per-channel webhooks.
The public inbound webhook `POST /api/v1/channels/webhooks/{id}/{token}` requires no user auth and supports custom name/avatar metadata, pushing live over socket.io.
The webhook path inserts and emits a message but does NOT auto-trigger OpenWebUI's model response handler, unlike a user `@`-mention.
A Pipe Function can register the fabric as a selectable, `@`-mentionable model that streams replies via HTTP/MCP.
Pipelines provide the same Pipe/Filter model out-of-process for heavy or long-running adapters.
Tools and Filters are treated as supporting roles, not primary conversation or inbound mechanisms.
A code fork is reserved only for custom panels/affordances that native Channels and plugins cannot express.
standouts: The undocumented channel inbound webhook is identified as the no-fork solution for inbound push from external agents.
Native Channels already supplies the members/channels panel, matching the fabric's own conceptual model.
The recommended spine is a fabric channel plus a fabric-as-model Pipe plus inbound webhook.
A strong version-divergence warning is issued because the verified source and the likely deploy image are not the same.
conventions: Uses evidence-grounded claims with explicit source file and line-number references.
Organizes content as TL;DR decision, detailed mechanism survey, deep-dive on Channels, and final ranked fit list.
Includes risk caveats and calls for smoke-testing on the exact deploy image.
Illustrates mechanisms with inline Python code snippets.
Employs a use-case-specific ranking table, while noting that a flat ranking would otherwise mislead.
concerns: The load-bearing inbound webhook is undocumented and could change across versions.
The deploy target image is unpinned (`:main`) while the evidence is pinned to v0.8.12, leaving a verification gap.
Line-number references will drift as the upstream source changes.
The final ranking, though use-case-framed, could be read as a universal ordering of OpenWebUI extension mechanisms.
notes: A decision-oriented research note that says OpenWebUI's native Channels + undocumented inbound webhook can satisfy the hardest fabric-integration requirements without a fork, but only if verified on the exact deployed version.
questions: Which exact image/version will be deployed, and does the channel webhook exist there?
Will the fabric expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, or does it require a Pipe/adapter?
How are webhook tokens rotated, revoked, and scoped beyond per-channel creation?
What is the path for adding fabric-specific UI affordances to the native Channels panel without a fork?
How does the system behave under concurrent/high-volume fabric agent posts via the webhook?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose matches content: it surveys OpenWebUI customization and extension options and recommends an integration architecture for wiring OpenWebUI to an external multi-agent fabric.
apparent_themes: Prefer native substrate over a fork when possible.
Ground claims in source verification rather than docs alone.
Pin versions and smoke-test undocumented capabilities.
Align the fabric's channel/member mental model with OpenWebUI's existing Channels feature.
Separate outbound, inbound, and panel concerns across distinct mechanisms.
intention_signals: The file is trying to guide a build decision that integrates an external multi-agent fabric into OpenWebUI with minimal source modification, while explicitly warning about version drift and undocumented APIs.
novelty: It identifies OpenWebUI's undocumented channel inbound webhook as a general external-agent push surface, enabling a real-time multi-agent channel experience without forking the codebase.
contribution: This file contributes a use-case-specific mapping of OpenWebUI extension layers, a recommended no-fork integration architecture (native Channels + inbound webhook + Pipe Function), and explicit version-drift and verification warnings that a fused system would keep as decision rationale.
summary_for_embedding: A research report evaluating OpenWebUI v0.8.12 customization and extension mechanisms for integration with an external multi-agent fabric, recommending native Channels, an undocumented inbound webhook, and a Pipe Function as the no-fork architecture.
intention_for_embedding: To inform a build decision for wiring OpenWebUI to an external fabric while warning about version divergence and undocumented APIs.
